title: Review: Breiman (2001) and Shmueli (2010) — Two Cultures, Two Goals
text: Two landmark papers in statistical and data science methodology, Leo Breiman’s “Statistical Modeling: The Two Cultures” (2001) and Galit Shmueli’s “To Explain or to Predict?” (2010) together define a foundational debate about the purpose of quantitative modeling. While Breiman focuses on the divide between statistical modeling traditions, Shmueli refines and extends this debate by drawing a principled distinction between explanation and prediction as distinct scientific goals. Read together, these papers provide an essential framework for understanding methodological choices in knowledge mining, machine learning, and social science research.

title: Review: Breiman (2001) and Shmueli (2010) — Two Cultures, Two Goals
text: Two landmark papers in statistical and data science methodology, Leo Breiman’s “Statistical Modeling: The Two Cultures” (2001) and Galit Shmueli’s “To Explain or to Predict?” (2010) together define a foundational debate about the purpose of quantitative modeling. While Breiman focuses on the divide between statistical modeling traditions, Shmueli refines and extends this debate by drawing a principled distinction between explanation and prediction as distinct scientific goals. Read together, these papers provide an essential framework for understanding methodological choices in knowledge mining, machine learning, and social science research.

title: Review: Breiman (2001) and Shmueli (2010) — Two Cultures, Two Goals
text:
Breiman (2001): The Two Cultures
Breiman’s central argument is that statisticians have historically operated within a single dominant paradigm, the data-modeling culture in which the analyst assumes a stochastic data-generating model (e.g., linear regression or logistic regression) and uses data to estimate its parameters. This culture prioritizes interpretability, inference, and hypothesis testing, but Breiman argues it has led statisticians to work on problems that are “irrelevant” to data-modeling culture in which the analyst assumes a stochastic data-generating model (e.g., linear or the real challenges of prediction and pattern recognition in complex data.
Against this, Breiman advocates for the algorithmic modeling culture, which treats the data-generating process as unknown and unknowable, and instead focuses on developing algorithms that produce accurate predictions from input data. Random forests, boosting, and neural networks exemplify this approach. Breiman presents evidence primarily from prediction competitions and practical applications that algorithmic models consistently outperform data models on predictive accuracy, particularly in high-dimensional settings.
Breiman’s critique is sharp: he argues that the statistics profession’s commitment to data models has led to a dangerous narrowing of focus, causing statisticians to miss the most important developments in machine learning and to provide poor guidance to applied researchers who need accurate predictions rather than elegant models.

Key Contributions

Introduced the influential “two cultures” framing that has shaped subsequent methodological debates
Provided empirical evidence for the predictive superiority of algorithmic models in applied settings
Challenged the statistics profession to broaden its methodological toolkit
Highlighted the tension between model interpretability and predictive accuracy



Limitations

Conflates the goal of prediction with the goal of understanding, treating predictive accuracy as the primary criterion for model evaluation, without adequately addressing when explanation matters
Provides limited guidance on how to choose between cultures for a given research problem
The “two cultures” framing is somewhat binary, obscuring the continuum of modeling approaches between the extremes

title: Review: Breiman (2001) and Shmueli (2010) — Two Cultures, Two Goals
section: Shmueli (2010): To Explain or to Predict?
text:
Shmueli (2010): To Explain or to Predict?
Where Breiman diagnoses a methodological divide, Shmueli provides a conceptual framework for resolving it. Her central contribution is the distinction between explanatory modeling and predictive modeling as distinct scientific activities with different goals, criteria, and appropriate methods.
Explanatory modeling uses statistical models to test causal hypotheses and estimate the strength of relationships between variables. The goal is to understand why an outcome occurs to identify the mechanisms that drive phenomena of interest. Theory guides variable selection, model specification, and interpretation. Fit to existing data (in-sample performance) is the primary criterion of success.
Predictive modeling uses statistical or algorithmic models to generate accurate forecasts for new, unseen observations. The goal is to know what will happen to produce reliable predictions that can inform decisions. Out-of-sample performance is the primary criterion of success, and theoretical interpretability is secondary.
Shmueli argues that these two goals require fundamentally different methodological choices at every stage of the research process, from variable selection and model specification to performance evaluation and validation. A model optimized for explanation may perform poorly for prediction, and vice versa. Crucially, she identifies a third category, descriptive modeling, which aims to summarize or represent data structure without causal or predictive intent.

Key Contributions

Provides a principled, operational distinction between explanation and prediction that Breiman’s framework lacks
Demonstrates that the explanation-prediction gap is not merely philosophical but has concrete methodological implications affecting variable selection, model evaluation, and validation strategies
Introduces the concept of predictive power as distinct from explanatory power, and shows that commonly used fit statistics (R², AIC) are not appropriate for evaluating predictive models
Argues that both goals are scientifically legitimate and that researchers should be explicit about which goal they are pursuing



Limitations

The distinction between explanation and prediction, while conceptually valuable, is harder to maintain in practice many research questions involve both goals simultaneously
Does not fully address how causal inference methods (e.g., instrumental variables, difference-in-differences) relate to her framework
The paper focuses primarily on regression-based settings and does not fully engage with the algorithmic modeling approaches that Breiman advocates

title: Review: Breiman (2001) and Shmueli (2010) — Two Cultures, Two Goals
text:
Comparative Analysis

Points of Agreement
Both papers agree that the statistics profession has been insufficiently attentive to the practical goal of accurate prediction. Breiman argues this has led statisticians to prefer elegant but poorly fitting models; Shmueli argues it has led researchers to conflate in-sample fit with out-of-sample predictive accuracy. Both call for greater methodological pluralism and more explicit attention to the goals that models are meant to serve.
Both papers also agree that model evaluation criteria should be matched to research goals. Breiman’s emphasis on cross-validation and out-of-sample testing aligns with Shmueli’s argument that predictive models must be evaluated on held-out data rather than in-sample fit statistics.


Points of Divergence
The most important difference between the two papers is in their treatment of explanation. Breiman is largely dismissive of explanatory modeling, treating predictive accuracy as the primary criterion of model quality and arguing that the search for interpretable mechanisms is often misguided. Shmueli, by contrast, treats explanation as a fully legitimate and irreducibly important scientific goal, one that is simply different from prediction, not inferior to it.
This difference has significant implications for social science research. In public administration and behavioral public administration, my own research area, the goal is typically to understand why citizens behave as they do toward government institutions, not merely to predict their behavior. A machine learning model that predicts citizen distrust with 90% accuracy but provides no insight into the mechanisms driving distrust is less useful for policy design than an explanatory model that identifies the causal pathways, even if the latter fits the data less well.
Shmueli’s framework is therefore more directly applicable to social science research than Breiman’s, because it provides principled guidance for choosing between methodological approaches based on research goals rather than treating predictive accuracy as universally paramount.


Complementarity
Despite their differences, the two papers are best read as complementary rather than contradictory. Breiman’s contribution is to establish that algorithmic modeling approaches deserve a central place in the analyst’s toolkit, that the assumption of a specific data-generating model is often unjustified, and that purely algorithmic approaches can deliver superior predictive performance. Shmueli’s contribution is to clarify when predictive accuracy is the right criterion and when it is not.
Together, they suggest a research design framework in which the analyst first asks: “Is my goal explanation or prediction?” If prediction, algorithmic methods, and cross-validated performance metrics are appropriate. If explanation, theory-driven model specification, causal identification strategies, and inferential statistics are appropriate. If, as is often the case in applied social science, the analyst must be transparent about the tradeoffs and report results from both perspectives.

title: Review: Breiman (2001) and Shmueli (2010) — Two Cultures, Two Goals
section: Implications for Knowledge Mining and Public Administration Research
text:
Implications for Knowledge Mining and Public Administration Research
For researchers working at the intersection of knowledge mining and public administration, these two papers define the methodological landscape:

Machine learning methods (random forests, gradient boosting, neural networks) excel at prediction but provide limited causal insight. They are appropriate for operational decision support, early warning systems, and pattern detection in large administrative datasets.
Regression-based explanatory models excel at testing theoretical hypotheses and estimating causal effects, but may sacrifice predictive accuracy. They are appropriate for policy evaluation, theory testing, and understanding mechanisms.
Hybrid approaches such as using machine learning for variable selection followed by regression-based estimation, or using causal forests for heterogeneous treatment effect estimation, increasingly bridge the two cultures.

The most important practical lesson from both papers is the one Shmueli states most clearly: researchers must be explicit about their goals before choosing their methods. Confusing explanation and prediction using in-sample fit to evaluate a predictive model, or interpreting a predictive model’s coefficients as causal effects, leads to methodological errors that undermine the validity of research conclusions.

title: Review: Breiman (2001) and Shmueli (2010) — Two Cultures, Two Goals
text:
Conclusion
Breiman (2001) and Shmueli (2010) together constitute a foundational dialogue about the purpose and practice of statistical modeling. Breiman disrupts the data modeling monoculture by demonstrating the power of algorithmic approaches; Shmueli provides the conceptual framework needed to deploy these approaches wisely. For researchers in knowledge mining and behavioral public administration, the core lesson is this: choose your methods based on your goals, be transparent about that choice, and do not mistake predictive accuracy for causal understanding or vice versa.

title: Review: Breiman (2001) and Shmueli (2010) — Two Cultures, Two Goals
text:
References

Breiman, L. (2001). Statistical modeling: The two cultures (with comments and a rejoinder by the author). Statistical Science, 16(3), 199–231.
Shmueli, G. (2010). To explain or to predict? Statistical Science, 25(3), 289–310.

title: Reflection 08 (Class 12): AI for Science and Agentic AI Research Workflows
text:
“AI for Science” refers to the deliberate application of artificial intelligence systems, including machine learning, natural language processing, and increasingly agentic AI, to accelerate, augment, and transform the core processes of scientific inquiry. It is distinct from AI as a research object (studying AI systems themselves) and from AI as a productivity tool (using AI to write emails or format documents). AI for Science treats AI as an active participant in the research process: generating hypotheses, designing experiments, analyzing data, synthesizing literature, and reasoning about uncertainty.
The concept gained institutional momentum with major investments from funding agencies and research institutions, including Google DeepMind’s AlphaFold, which predicted protein structures at a scale and accuracy previously unimaginable, and Microsoft’s partnership with OpenAI to build AI-assisted research platforms. In social science and public administration, AI for Science is emerging more cautiously but with significant potential, particularly for analyzing administrative data at scale, synthesizing interdisciplinary literature, and modeling complex behavioral phenomena.
At its core, AI for Science rests on three premises:

Scale: Scientific data, genomic sequences, climate records, administrative archives, and social media corpora have grown beyond the capacity of human researchers to process manually. AI systems can operate at scales that are qualitatively inaccessible to unaided human cognition.
Pattern discovery: AI systems can identify subtle, high-dimensional patterns in complex data that human researchers might not think to look for, generating hypotheses that can then be tested through conventional scientific methods.
Acceleration: AI can compress research timelines, reducing the time from data collection to insight, from hypothesis to experimental test, from literature search to synthesis, enabling faster iteration across the scientific cycle.

title: Reflection 06 (Class 10): Retrieval-Augmented Generation and Its Limitations
text:
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is an AI architecture that combines the generative capabilities of large language models with an external retrieval mechanism to ground model outputs in verifiable, up-to-date documents. Introduced by Lewis et al. (2020), RAG addresses one of the most critical limitations of standard LLMs: the inability to access information beyond their training data and the tendency to hallucinate when queried on knowledge-intensive topics.
The RAG pipeline operates in two stages:

Retrieval: When a query is submitted, the system searches an external knowledge base, a document corpus, an academic database, or a web index using dense vector retrieval or sparse keyword matching to identify the most relevant passages or documents.
Generation: The retrieved documents are passed to the LLM as context alongside the original query. The model generates a response that is grounded in the retrieved material rather than relying solely on parametric knowledge encoded in its weights.



RAG is particularly consequential for academic research for several interconnected reasons:
1. Factual grounding and citation traceability: Standard LLMs frequently hallucinate citations, generating plausible-sounding but nonexistent references. RAG systems that retrieve from indexed academic databases (e.g., Semantic Scholar, PubMed, arXiv) can ground responses in real documents, making citations verifiable. For researchers who use AI to assist with literature synthesis, this fundamentally changes the reliability of the output.
2. Access to current knowledge: LLMs have a training cutoff; they cannot access publications, policy documents, or data released after training. RAG overcomes this by retrieving from live or regularly updated corpora, enabling AI systems to engage with the most recent scholarship. For a fast-moving field like computational social science or AI governance, this is essential.
3. Domain adaptation without retraining: RAG allows LLMs to operate effectively in specialized domains legal documents, medical records, government administrative data without the cost of full model retraining or fine-tuning. Researchers can build domain-specific RAG systems over their own document collections, enabling AI assistance that is tailored to their specific research context.
4. Transparency and auditability: Because RAG responses are grounded in retrieved documents, researchers can inspect the source material that informed a given output. This increases the auditability of AI-assisted research and supports the kind of transparent methodology documentation that academic integrity requires.
5. Implications for public administration research: In my own research on public trust and e-government, a RAG system built over a corpus of government policy documents, citizen feedback archives, and public administration literature could enable systematic analysis of how institutional language and policy framing have evolved while keeping outputs traceable to specific source documents rather than generalized model outputs.



The fundamental difference between a standard LLM response and a RAG-grounded response is the epistemological status of the output. A standard LLM generates text by predicting statistically likely continuations based on patterns in training data it has no mechanism for distinguishing between accurate recall and plausible confabulation. A RAG system, by contrast, generates text in the context of specific retrieved passages, making the basis for the response explicit and checkable.
This does not make RAG outputs fully trustworthy the retrieval system may return irrelevant or low-quality documents, the model may misinterpret retrieved passages, or the underlying corpus may itself contain errors. But it substantially raises the epistemic floor by making AI outputs falsifiable: a researcher can check whether the retrieved documents actually support the claim the model has made.

title: Reflection 06 (Class 10): Retrieval-Augmented Generation and Its Limitations
section: Recent criticisms of RAG and suggestions for improvement
text:
Recent criticisms of RAG and suggestions for improvement

Criticisms
Despite its advantages over standard LLMs, RAG has attracted significant criticism as it has been more widely deployed and scrutinized:
1. Retrieval quality bottleneck; RAG is only as good as its retrieval component. If the retrieval system fails to surface the most relevant documents due to vocabulary mismatch, poor embedding quality, or inadequate corpus coverage the generator receives unhelpful context and may produce worse outputs than a standard LLM that relies on its parametric knowledge. Studies have shown that RAG can actively hurt performance when retrieved documents are irrelevant or contradictory to the query.
2. Context window limitations: Retrieved documents must fit within the LLM’s context window. For long documents or queries requiring synthesis across many sources, this creates a hard constraint on how much retrieved information can inform a single generation step. Important passages may be truncated or excluded, leading to incomplete or biased responses.
3. Faithfulness vs. groundedness tension; RAG systems face a fundamental tension: the model should be faithful to retrieved documents (not contradicting them) and grounded in them (using them as the primary basis for the response). In practice, models often blend parametric knowledge with retrieved information in ways that are difficult to disentangle, producing outputs that are partially grounded but not transparently so.
4. Multi-hop reasoning failures: Many research questions require synthesizing information across multiple documents through chains of reasoning for example, tracing how a policy concept evolved across ten years of government reports. Standard RAG architectures retrieve documents independently and pass them to the generator as a flat context, making complex multi-hop reasoning difficult. The model may identify relevant passages in individual documents but fail to connect them into coherent analytical conclusions.
5. Corpus quality and bias: RAG systems inherit the biases of their retrieval corpus. If the indexed documents over-represent certain perspectives, time periods, geographic regions, or methodological traditions, the system’s outputs will reflect those imbalances potentially in ways that are less visible than the biases of a standard LLM, since the retrieved documents create an illusion of empirical grounding.
6. Computational cost: RAG systems require maintaining and indexing large document corpora, running dense retrieval at inference time, and passing large contexts to the generator. This substantially increases the computational and financial cost of AI-assisted research compared to direct LLM querying, which may limit accessibility for researchers without institutional infrastructure.



Suggestions for improving RAG
1. Iterative and adaptive retrieval: Rather than retrieving once at the start of a query, iterative RAG architectures retrieve multiple times during generation using intermediate outputs to refine subsequent retrievals. This improves multi-hop reasoning and allows the system to progressively narrow in on relevant information.
2. Re-ranking and relevance filtering: Adding a re-ranking step between retrieval and generation using a cross-encoder model to score retrieved passages by relevance substantially improves the quality of the context passed to the generator and reduces the noise introduced by irrelevant documents.
3. Self-RAG and reflective generation: Self-RAG (Asai et al., 2023) trains models to decide when retrieval is needed, evaluate the relevance of retrieved passages, and assess whether their outputs are grounded, generating explicit reflection tokens that make the model’s epistemic state transparent. This addresses both the retrieval quality bottleneck and the faithfulness tension.
4. Knowledge graph integration: Combining RAG with structured knowledge graphs enables more reliable multi-hop reasoning by providing explicit relational structures that the model can traverse, rather than requiring it to infer relationships from unstructured retrieved passages.
5. Corpus curation and bias auditing: Research RAG systems should be built on carefully curated, bias-audited corpora with transparent documentation of coverage, inclusion criteria, and known limitations. For academic research, this means preferring indexed peer-reviewed databases over open web retrieval and documenting corpus composition as part of the research methods.
6. Hybrid parametric-retrieval architectures: Future RAG systems should more explicitly manage the boundary between parametric knowledge (when the model should rely on what it learned during training) and retrieved knowledge (when it should defer to external documents), rather than blending them implicitly. This would improve both transparency and factual accuracy.

title: Reflection 04 (Class 8): NLP, LLMs, and Knowledge Mining
text:
Text mining enables the systematic analysis of large corpora that would be impossible to read manually. In knowledge mining, this creates several analytical capabilities that close reading fundamentally cannot match:
Scale: Text mining can process thousands or millions of documents simultaneously — congressional records spanning decades, social media streams from millions of users, or archives of government reports. Close reading is cognitively constrained to hundreds of texts at most.
Pattern detection across corpora: Techniques such as topic modeling (LDA), word embeddings, and keyword co-occurrence analysis reveal latent themes and structural patterns that are invisible when reading documents individually. A researcher reading DOD China reports one by one may sense a rhetorical shift, but text scaling methods like Wordfish can precisely quantify when and how that shift occurred.
Temporal trends: Text mining tracks how language, framing, and issue salience evolve across time in ways that close reading cannot systematically capture.
Replicability: Text mining produces transparent, reproducible results that can be audited and replicated. Close reading interpretations are inherently subjective and researcher-dependent.




Loss of context: Bag-of-words and frequency-based models strip away sentence structure, irony, sarcasm, and pragmatic meaning that close readers naturally capture
Cultural and linguistic nuance: Word embeddings trained on English corpora perform poorly on non-Western contexts, multilingual texts, or domain-specific jargon
Normative judgment: Text mining can identify that a term appears frequently but cannot determine whether its use is appropriate, harmful, or significant without human interpretation
Preprocessing sensitivity: Analytical results are highly sensitive to tokenization, stopword removal, and stemming decisions — choices that are often arbitrary and underdocumented
Hallucinated patterns: Unsupervised methods can generate statistically coherent but substantively meaningless patterns, especially with noisy or small corpora

title: Reflection 04 (Class 8): NLP, LLMs, and Knowledge Mining
section: How NLP and LLMs can assist my research
text:
How NLP and LLMs can assist my research
My research focuses on public trust in government, e-government adoption, and behavioral public administration. NLP and LLMs offer several specific analytical opportunities:

Specific examples
1. Automated analysis of citizen feedback and e-government reviews Large volumes of unstructured citizen feedback from government service portals, app store reviews, and social media can be analyzed using sentiment analysis and aspect-based opinion mining. For example, NLP pipelines using BERT-based models can classify citizen reviews of digital tax filing systems by sentiment and identify which service dimensions (speed, transparency, usability) drive satisfaction or distrust — at a scale impossible with manual coding.
2. Topic modeling of government policy documents LDA or structural topic models applied to a corpus of government digital transformation policy documents can reveal how policy framing has shifted over time — for example, whether discourse has moved from efficiency-focused to trust-focused language. This directly informs my research on institutional communication and public trust.
3. LLMs as coding assistants for survey open-ended responses In behavioral public administration research, surveys often include open-ended questions asking citizens to describe their experiences with government services. LLMs can assist with qualitative coding by generating initial category labels, which human researchers then verify — substantially reducing the time cost of qualitative analysis while preserving interpretive rigor.
4. Literature synthesis and gap identification LLMs like Claude and ChatGPT can rapidly synthesize large bodies of literature, identify conceptual gaps, and generate structured literature maps. For a PhD researcher navigating an interdisciplinary field spanning public administration, behavioral science, and information systems, this is a powerful scoping tool.
5. Simulation of citizen responses for pre-testing survey instruments LLMs can simulate how diverse population segments might interpret and respond to survey questions, enabling pre-testing of instrument clarity and item bias before fielding expensive surveys.

title: Reflection 04 (Class 8): NLP, LLMs, and Knowledge Mining
text:
Limitations and solutions







Limitation
Solution




Hallucination: LLMs generate confident but factually incorrect claims
Always verify citations and factual claims against primary sources; use RAG systems that ground outputs in retrievable documents


Training data bias: Models trained predominantly on Western, English-language text
Use domain-adapted or multilingual models; critically examine outputs for cultural and linguistic bias


Lack of causal reasoning: LLMs identify associations, not causal relationships
Combine LLM-assisted text analysis with causal inference designs (IV, DiD, RCT)


Reproducibility: LLM outputs vary across runs and model versions
Document model version, temperature settings, and prompts; use deterministic settings where possible


Data privacy: Submitting sensitive research data to commercial LLM APIs
Use locally deployed open-source models (LLaMA, Mistral) for sensitive data; anonymize inputs


Evaluation difficulty: Hard to assess output quality without domain expertise
Always include human expert review; use established benchmarks for standardized tasks

title: Reflection 04 (Class 8): NLP, LLMs, and Knowledge Mining
section: AI for research guide: ethical, technical, and academic concerns
text:
AI for research guide: ethical, technical, and academic concerns
The following guide addresses the responsible use of AI in academic research, incorporating the concerns raised by AI safety researchers, including Geoffrey Hinton, who has warned that AI systems may generate outputs that appear authoritative while being fundamentally unreliable.

Before using AI

Define the task clearly. AI performs best on well-specified tasks. Vague prompts produce generic outputs that may introduce bias into your research framework before you have critically evaluated it.
Understand what you are asking. If you cannot evaluate the quality of an AI output in a given domain, you should not use AI for that task without expert verification.
Check your institution’s policy. Many universities have policies on AI use in coursework and research. Using AI without disclosure may constitute academic misconduct.



During the use: Technical concerns

Never trust citations without verification. LLMs frequently hallucinate references that appear real but do not exist. Every citation must be verified against Google Scholar, Semantic Scholar, or the original publication.
Treat outputs as drafts, not final products. AI-generated text requires critical editing for accuracy, nuance, and academic voice. Submitting unedited AI output as your own work is both academically dishonest and intellectually risky.
Be aware of version sensitivity. The same prompt submitted to different model versions or at different temperatures will produce different outputs. Document your AI interactions if they inform your research.
Avoid automation bias. Research shows that humans tend to over-trust automated outputs. Maintain active skepticism toward AI-generated analysis, especially when it confirms your prior expectations.



Ethical concerns

Transparency and disclosure. Clearly disclose when and how AI tools were used in your research. Failure to disclose constitutes a form of academic deception, even if AI use is not explicitly prohibited.
Authorship and attribution. AI cannot be listed as an author. All intellectual responsibility for research outputs rests with the human researcher. If AI substantially contributed to a text, that contribution must be disclosed in the methods section.
Bias amplification. AI models trained on historical data encode historical biases. Using AI to generate literature summaries or code qualitative data without bias auditing can systematically distort your findings.
Environmental cost. Training and querying large AI models has substantial energy and carbon costs. Researchers should consider whether AI use is proportionate to the analytical need.



The Hinton warning: When AI creates more problems than solutions
Geoffrey Hinton, one of the foundational figures in deep learning, resigned from Google in 2023 to speak freely about the risks of advanced AI systems. His concerns are directly relevant to academic research:

Epistemic authority without epistemic accountability: LLMs produce authoritative-sounding outputs without any mechanism for accountability when they are wrong. In research, this can corrupt the knowledge base if AI-generated claims are not rigorously verified before publication.
Homogenization of knowledge: If researchers across disciplines use the same AI models to generate literature reviews, hypotheses, and analytical frameworks, the diversity of intellectual perspectives in academia may narrow — a form of epistemic monoculture.
Speed-accuracy tradeoff: AI tools accelerate research processes in ways that may outpace researchers’ capacity for critical evaluation, creating incentives to publish AI-assisted work that has not been sufficiently scrutinized.
Irreversibility: Once AI-generated errors enter the published literature, they are difficult to correct and may propagate through citation networks — a problem that is qualitatively different from traditional human error.

The fundamental principle: AI tools in research should augment human judgment, not replace it. The researcher remains intellectually responsible for every claim in their work, regardless of how it was generated.

title: Reflection 02 (Class 6): ML Pipeline, Error Analysis, and Overfitting
text: Error analysis

title: Reflection 02 (Class 6): ML Pipeline, Error Analysis, and Overfitting
section: The use of accuracy alone and overfitting to knowledge vs. noise
text:
The use of accuracy alone and overfitting to knowledge vs. noise

Error analysis
Accuracy alone is an incomplete measure of model performance, particularly when classes are imbalanced or when the costs of different error types differ.
In this pipeline, the TEDS 2016 dataset has an imbalanced vote distribution: approximately 63% of respondents voted for Tsai and 37% for other candidates. A naive model that always predicted “Tsai” would achieve 63% accuracy without learning anything meaningful. Accuracy alone would not expose this failure.
Error analysis through the confusion matrix reveals two types of errors:

False Positives (Type I Error): Predicting “Tsai” when the respondent voted for another candidate
False Negatives (Type II Error): Predicting “Other” when the respondent actually voted for Tsai

The sensitivity and specificity from the confusion matrix reveal that the three models differ meaningfully in how they distribute these errors, even when their overall accuracy is similar. The decision tree showed higher sensitivity but lower specificity than logistic regression, meaning it was better at identifying Tsai voters but more likely to misclassify non-Tsai voters. This tradeoff is invisible if we only examine accuracy.
Kappa, which corrects for chance agreement, provides a more honest assessment of model performance than raw accuracy under class imbalance.


Overfitting: knowledge vs. noise
Overfitting occurs when a model learns the noise and idiosyncratic patterns in the training data rather than the underlying generalizable signal. An overfit model performs well on training data but poorly on unseen test data.

Knowledge refers to stable, generalizable patterns the real relationships between variables and outcomes that hold across contexts (e.g., DPP partisanship predicts Tsai support)
Noise refers to random variation, measurement error, and sample-specific quirks that do not generalize

An overfit model mistakes noise for knowledge. Cross-validation, regularization, and ensemble methods like random forests address overfitting by averaging across multiple models, reducing variance without sacrificing too much bias. In this pipeline, the random forest’s ensemble approach makes it more robust to overfitting than the single decision tree, evident in its higher test accuracy and more balanced error profile.

title: Reflection (Class 4): Using AI for Research
text:
Starting to use AI effectively for academic research requires a deliberate and structured approach not simply opening a chatbot and asking it to “write my literature review.” Based on my experience across this course, I have developed the following workflow for integrating AI into my research on public trust, e-government adoption, and behavioral public administration.
Step 1: Define the task before opening the tool. AI performs best when given specific, well-scoped tasks. Before using any AI tool, I identify precisely what I need a summary of a specific paper, help debugging R code, a first draft of a conceptual framework, or suggestions for search terms. Vague requests produce generic outputs; specific requests produce useful ones.
Step 2: Use AI for literature scoping, not literature replacement. I use AI models to identify relevant bodies of literature and key concepts, then verify every suggestion against Google Scholar or Semantic Scholar before including it in my work. AI is useful for mapping a field I am unfamiliar with it is not a substitute for reading primary sources.
Step 3: Use AI for code assistance and debugging. For my quantitative work in R — SEM, hierarchical regression, and scale reliability analysis, AI significantly accelerates the coding process. I describe what I want to accomplish analytically, ask the model to suggest the appropriate R functions and syntax, then verify and adapt the output based on my own understanding of the analysis.
Step 4: Use AI for drafting, not writing. I use AI to generate first drafts of sections I find difficult to start — introductions, transitions, method descriptions then substantially revise them in my own voice. I never submit AI-generated text without significant editing and critical evaluation.
Step 5: Verify everything independently. Every factual claim, citation, and analytical conclusion generated with AI assistance is independently verified before it enters my research. AI tools are powerful but unreliable narrators they hallucinate confidently and require human oversight at every step.

title: Reflection (Class 4): Using AI for Research
section: Q2. Which Two AI Models Are You Using and How Can You Leverage Them?
text:
Q2. Which Two AI Models Are You Using and How Can You Leverage Them?

Model 1: Claude (Anthropic)
I use Claude primarily for literature synthesis, conceptual framework development, and writing assistance. Claude’s ability to engage with long, complex documents and maintain coherent reasoning across extended conversations makes it particularly well-suited for the kind of interdisciplinary synthesis my research requires connecting behavioral public administration theory with technology adoption models, institutional trust theory, and algorithmic governance scholarship.
Specific use cases in my research:

Literature mapping: I describe my research questions and ask Claude to identify the key theoretical traditions, foundational papers, and recent developments I should engage with. I then verify these suggestions against database searches.
Conceptual framework development: When developing my Algorithmic Accountability Framework (AAF) for the MiDAS case, I used Claude to stress-test the logical structure of my framework, asking it to identify gaps, alternative explanations, and potential objections that I then addressed in my writing.
Writing assistance: I use Claude to generate first drafts of difficult sections, which I then revise substantially. Claude is particularly useful for translating complex methodological decisions into accessible prose for non-specialist readers.
Code debugging: For my R analyses using SEM and scale reliability methods, Claude helps me debug error messages and suggests alternative analytical approaches when my initial specification produces unexpected results.

Limitations I have encountered: Claude occasionally generates plausible-sounding but unverifiable citations. I have learned to always verify references independently and to prompt Claude explicitly to flag uncertainty rather than generate confident but potentially incorrect claims.



Model 2: ChatGPT (OpenAI)
I use ChatGPT primarily for quantitative analysis support, data exploration, and prompt iteration. ChatGPT’s Code Interpreter (Advanced Data Analysis) feature is particularly useful for exploratory data analysis and visualization when I want to quickly inspect a dataset before conducting more rigorous analysis in R.
Specific use cases in my research:

Exploratory data analysis: I upload datasets, including my federal AI governance dataset built from OMB Consolidated AI Use Case Inventories, and use ChatGPT’s code interpreter to generate quick summaries, missingness assessments, and preliminary visualizations that inform my subsequent R analysis.
Survey instrument development: I use ChatGPT to generate alternative phrasings for survey items, evaluate potential sources of item bias, and simulate how different respondent populations might interpret ambiguous questions.
Prompt refinement: As demonstrated in Assignment 02, I use ChatGPT as one of three models for systematic literature review tasks, leveraging its broad domain coverage while compensating for its tendency toward shallow synthesis with more specific prompts.
Data cleaning logic: For complex data cleaning tasks, such as recoding variables, handling missing data patterns, and merging datasets, I describe my data structure and cleaning goals to ChatGPT and use its suggested code as a starting point for my R workflow.

Limitations I have encountered: ChatGPT tends toward breadth over depth in literature synthesis tasks, and its citations require more rigorous verification than Claude’s. Its responses are also more sensitive to prompt wording, requiring more iteration to obtain academically rigorous outputs.

title: Reflection (Class 4): Using AI for Research
text:
Leveraging Both Models Strategically
The most effective approach is to use Claude and ChatGPT in complementary roles rather than treating them as interchangeable. Claude excels at sustained, nuanced reasoning across complex interdisciplinary material, which is my primary tool for conceptual work. ChatGPT excels at quick exploratory analysis and broad scoping. It is my primary tool for data work and initial literature mapping.
For any given research task, I typically begin with ChatGPT for broad scoping and exploratory analysis, then use Claude for deeper synthesis and framework development. I verify all outputs independently and treat both models as powerful but fallible assistants rather than authoritative sources.

title: Assignment 03: ML Exercise
text:
The problem
The problem is that the dependent variable votetsai is binary (0 = voted for other candidate, 1 = voted for Tsai Ing-wen). Linear regression assumes the outcome is continuous, so applying it to a binary variable produces several issues:

The regression line can predict values outside the range of 0 and 1, which are not valid probabilities
The relationship between predictors and a binary outcome is inherently non-linear
The assumption of normally distributed residuals is violated since the DV only takes two values
This makes the regression line a poor and misleading fit, as visible in the plots above, where data points cluster at 0 and 1 but the fitted line cuts through the middle

title: Assignment 03: ML Exercise
text:
Measures to improve prediction
Since the dependent variable is binary, logistic regression is the appropriate model. It models the log-odds of the outcome and constrains all predictions between 0 and 1.

logit_model <- glm(votetsai ~ age + edu + income,
                   data = TEDS_2016,

Assignment 01: Brush-up R and Quarto
section: Problems encountered when working with the dataset
text:
Problems encountered when working with the dataset
Several issues arise when working with the TEDS 2016 dataset:

Missing values: Several variables contain a large number of NA values, particularly vote-related variables such as votetsai, votekmt, and income-related variables.
Labelled variables: The dataset is imported from Stata (.dta), so many variables come in as labelled class rather than standard R factor or numeric types, requiring conversion before analysis.
Categorical variables stored as numeric: Variables like Tondu, Party, Sex, and Edu are coded as integers but represent categorical categories, which can lead to incorrect analysis if treated as continuous.
Skewed distributions: Some variables such as income and age may have skewed distributions that need to be examined before modeling.



Code
# Check missing values across all variables
TEDS_2016 %>%
  summarise(across(everything(), ~sum(is.na(.)))) %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(),
               names_to = "variable",
               values_to = "n_missing") %>%
  filter(n_missing > 0) %>%
  arrange(desc(n_missing))

title: Assignment 01: Brush-up R and Quarto
text:
Deal with missing values
There are several strategies for handling missing values depending on context:

Listwise deletion (drop_na()): Remove all rows with any missing values. Simple but can reduce sample size significantly.
Variable-wise filtering: Remove only rows where the specific variable of interest is missing, preserving more data for other analyses.
Imputation: Replace missing values with the mean, median, or mode for numeric variables, or use multiple imputation for more rigorous analysis.
Flagging: Create a binary indicator variable to flag whether a value was missing, which can itself be informative in modeling.

For this analysis, listwise deletion is used for modeling tasks, while missing values are retained for descriptive summaries.


Code
# Example: drop rows missing votetsai and key predictors
teds_clean <- TEDS_2016 %>%
  dplyr::select(votetsai, Tondu, female, DPP, age, income, edu, Taiwanese, Econ_worse) %>%
  drop_na()

cat("Rows after dropping missing values:", nrow(teds_clean), "\n")

title: Assignment 02: Prompt Exercise
text: This assignment designs prompts to conduct a structured systematic literature review on data mining and machine learning using multiple AI models (ChatGPT, Copilot, and Grok 3), analyzes each model’s contributions, and refines the prompts for academic precision and depth.

title: Assignment 02: Prompt Exercise
text: This assignment designs prompts to conduct a structured systematic literature review on data mining and machine learning using multiple AI models (ChatGPT, Copilot, and Grok 3), analyzes each model’s contributions, and refines the prompts for academic precision and depth.

title: Assignment 02: Prompt Exercise
text:
Initial prompt creation
The following baseline prompt was submitted to ChatGPT, Copilot, and Grok 3:

“Conduct a 2,000-word structured, systematic literature review on the applications of data mining and machine learning in real-world domains. Include a methodology section, synthesize key findings, identify trends and gaps, and propose one testable hypothesis. Use an academic tone and emulate systematic review standards.”

title: Assignment 02: Prompt Exercise
text:
Analyze model responses

ChatGPT output
ChatGPT produced a broadly structured response covering six real-world domains: healthcare, finance, manufacturing, marketing, public sector, and environmental science. It followed a systematic review format with clearly labeled sections including Introduction, Methodology, Domain Applications, Synthesis, Trends, Research Gaps, Hypothesis, Discussion, and Conclusion.
Strengths:

Comprehensive multi-domain coverage across six application areas
Detailed methodology section with explicit inclusion/exclusion criteria and a study selection process (screening ~500 articles down to ~60)
Well-articulated discussion section connecting findings to broader implications
Proposed hypothesis focused on domain expertise integration with ML — novel and testable
Included hyperlinked citations to ScienceDirect, PubMed, and BMC Public Health

Weaknesses:

Some citations were hyperlinks rather than fully formatted APA references
Synthesis section was organized thematically (predictive analytics dominance, algorithmic trends) but lacked depth in domain-specific comparisons
Trends section included a non-English word (“গুরুত্বপূর্ণ”) suggesting an error in text generation
References section was minimal with only four sources listed despite citing multiple sources inline




Copilot output
Copilot delivered the most formally structured response, explicitly modeling the review after PRISMA standards and covering six domains: healthcare, finance, business/marketing, transportation, education, and public administration.
Strengths:

Most rigorous methodology section — explicitly referenced Moher et al. (2009) PRISMA guidelines
Included an abstract summarizing the full review
Well-structured cross-cutting trends section covering algorithmic evolution, data characteristics, and XAI
Clearly identified four research gaps with academic precision
Proposed hypothesis is specific, empirically testable, and linked directly to the identified gaps
References followed a consistent academic citation style

Weaknesses:

Word count appeared slightly below 2,000 words for the main body
Domain coverage, while systematic, was less deep than ChatGPT or Grok 3 in specific application examples
Limited domain diversity — did not cover agriculture, cybersecurity, or environmental applications
Discussion section was brief compared to other models




Grok 3 output
Grok 3 produced the most comprehensive and empirically detailed response, covering eight domains: healthcare, finance, retail, agriculture, manufacturing, transportation, education, and cybersecurity. It adhered explicitly to PRISMA 2020 guidelines and reported the most specific performance metrics.
Strengths:

Most empirically detailed — reported specific metrics (AUROCs >0.85, MSE reductions of 91.6%, 14–20% efficiency gains)
Broadest domain coverage with eight application areas including agriculture and cybersecurity
Explicitly referenced PRISMA 2020 (most recent version) and quality assessment using CASP checklists
Strongest trends section — covered DL dominance, IoT integration, generative AI, MLOps, XAI, and multimodal fusion
Most specific and falsifiable hypothesis — proposed a randomized controlled trial design with a 30% adoption rate threshold
Word count precisely met at 1,998 words

Weaknesses:

References section was minimal — only two sources cited formally, with others described as “available upon request”
Some claims (e.g., “Initial retrieval exceeded 5,000 records”) appear simulated rather than drawn from actual database searches
Writing style was occasionally dense and less accessible than Copilot’s output
Some domain sections (transportation, education) were shorter than others, creating uneven depth

title: Assignment 02: Prompt Exercise
text:
Refined prompts
Based on the weaknesses identified in each model’s response, the following tailored prompts were developed:

Refined prompt for ChatGPT

“Write a 2,000-word structured, systematic literature review on the applications of data mining and machine learning in healthcare, finance, manufacturing, and at least two additional domains. Follow PRISMA guidelines for the methodology section. For each domain, synthesize at least three peer-reviewed findings published between 2018 and 2024. Identify two major research gaps and propose one specific, testable hypothesis. Ensure all citations follow APA 7th edition format and are verifiable on Google Scholar or Semantic Scholar. Do not include any non-English text or placeholder citations.”

Improvement rationale: Adds APA citation requirements and verifiability standards to address ChatGPT’s incomplete references and the non-English text error. Specifies a publication date range and minimum findings per domain to improve synthesis depth.



Refined prompt for Copilot

“Produce a 2,000-word systematic literature review on data mining and machine learning applications across at least six domains, including healthcare, finance, business, transportation, education, and one emerging domain such as agriculture or cybersecurity. Use PRISMA methodology. Synthesize key findings in depth for each domain with specific performance metrics where available. Identify at least three meaningful research gaps, propose a testable hypothesis that spans multiple domains, and include a substantive discussion section. Maintain a rigorous academic tone throughout and provide a full reference list in APA format.”

Improvement rationale: Expands domain coverage to include emerging areas, requires specific performance metrics, and mandates a fuller reference list and discussion section to address Copilot’s brevity in these areas.



Refined prompt for Grok 3

“Conduct a 2,000-word systematic literature review on data mining and machine learning applications following PRISMA 2020 guidelines. Cover at least six real-world domains with empirically specific findings, including performance metrics. Highlight emerging trends such as federated learning, explainable AI, and generative AI. Identify critical research gaps with evidence. Propose one bold, falsifiable hypothesis with a clearly specified research design. Maintain a rigorous academic tone and provide a complete reference list in APA 7th edition format with at least ten verifiable sources — do not describe references as ‘available upon request.’”

Improvement rationale: Retains Grok 3’s strength in empirical detail and emerging trends while directly addressing its minimal reference list. Requires a minimum of ten verifiable APA citations to ensure academic rigor.



Comparison of refined outputs
After rerunning the refined prompts, all three models showed measurable improvement:

ChatGPT produced domain-specific synthesis with properly formatted APA citations and no language errors
Copilot expanded domain coverage to include agriculture and cybersecurity, added specific performance metrics, and produced a fuller reference list
Grok 3 maintained its empirical depth and innovative trends coverage while providing a complete, verifiable reference list

title: Assignment 02: Prompt Exercise
text:
Cross-Model collaboration

Synthesis prompt
The following prompt was used to integrate the best elements from all three model outputs:

“Using the following drafts from three AI models [ChatGPT output, Copilot output, Grok 3 output], produce a 2,000-word structured systematic literature review on data mining and machine learning applications. Combine the strongest methodology from Copilot (PRISMA-based, rigorous), the broad domain coverage and discussion depth from ChatGPT, and the empirical specificity and emerging trends insights from Grok 3. Produce a cohesive, academically sound document with a complete APA reference list and one bold, falsifiable hypothesis with a specified research design.”



Final synthesized systematic literature review

Abstract
Data mining (DM) and machine learning (ML) have emerged as foundational technologies across diverse real-world domains, enabling predictive analytics, pattern recognition, and decision support at scale. This systematic literature review, guided by PRISMA 2020 principles, synthesizes peer-reviewed research published between 2015 and 2024 on the application of DM and ML in healthcare, finance, manufacturing, retail, education, public administration, agriculture, and cybersecurity. Findings demonstrate high predictive accuracy and operational efficiencies across domains, with ensemble models and deep learning architectures consistently outperforming traditional statistical methods. Key trends include the growing adoption of explainable AI (XAI), federated learning, and generative AI. Persistent gaps include limited model generalizability, inadequate governance frameworks, and insufficient real-world deployment evaluation. A testable hypothesis linking XAI adoption to clinician trust and model adoption rates is proposed for future empirical investigation.


1. Introduction
Data mining and machine learning have transformed how organizations extract knowledge from large, complex datasets. While data mining focuses on discovering patterns and associations, machine learning emphasizes algorithmic learning from data to improve predictive performance over time (Han, Kamber, & Pei, 2012). In practice, the distinction between DM and ML has blurred, with modern applications integrating statistical learning, neural networks, and artificial intelligence techniques into unified analytical frameworks.
The real-world relevance of DM and ML has expanded alongside the digitization of healthcare records, financial transactions, social interactions, sensor data, and administrative processes. Organizations leverage ML models to detect fraud, predict disease outcomes, optimize logistics, personalize marketing, and inform public policy. Despite extensive research output, the literature remains fragmented across domains and methodological traditions, limiting cross-sectoral synthesis.
This review addresses three research objectives: (1) to systematically synthesize empirical applications of DM and ML across major real-world domains; (2) to identify dominant methodological approaches, trends, and research gaps; and (3) to propose a theory-driven, empirically testable hypothesis for future research.


2. Methodology
This study follows PRISMA 2020 guidelines (Page et al., 2021), emphasizing transparency, replicability, and structured synthesis. A structured search was conducted across PubMed, Scopus, Web of Science, IEEE Xplore, and Google Scholar using Boolean search strings combining domain-neutral and application-specific terms including “data mining,” “machine learning,” “real-world applications,” “healthcare,” “finance,” “education,” and “systematic review.” Searches were limited to English-language peer-reviewed publications between 2015 and 2024.
Inclusion criteria: Empirical studies applying DM or ML to real-world datasets; clear description of methods and application context; reported evaluation metrics or outcomes; peer-reviewed journal articles or conference proceedings.
Exclusion criteria: Purely theoretical or algorithmic papers without application; editorials, tutorials, and non-peer-reviewed sources; studies using synthetic data only.
Initial retrieval yielded approximately 1,200 records. After duplicate removal, title and abstract screening, and full-text review, 123 articles were retained for qualitative synthesis. Quality assessment used adapted CASP checklists focusing on data sources, validation procedures, and bias reporting.


3. Key findings by domain
Healthcare emerged as the most extensively studied domain. ML applications are primarily targeted at disease diagnosis, prognosis, resource allocation, and treatment recommendations. Random forests (42%), logistic regression (37%), and support vector machines (32%) were predominant for disease prediction, achieving AUROCs above 0.85 in cardiovascular, oncology, and diabetes applications (Alhumaidi et al., 2025). Deep learning models achieved radiologist-level accuracy in medical imaging tasks, including tumor detection and diabetic retinopathy screening (Rajpurkar et al., 2022). Challenges included data privacy concerns, heterogeneity of medical data, and limited model interpretability.
Finance and Banking applications focused predominantly on fraud detection, credit scoring, algorithmic trading, and risk assessment. Ensemble methods, including gradient boosting and random forests, consistently outperformed traditional statistical models, with multimodal fusion approaches reducing mean squared error by up to 91.6% in stock market forecasting (Grok 3 synthesis, 2025). Regulatory compliance and model transparency remained persistent barriers to deployment (Dal Pozzolo et al., 2015).
Manufacturing and Industry 4.0 leveraged ML for predictive maintenance, quality control, and supply chain optimization. Deep learning models mapped sensor data to fault detection, with studies reporting 10–15% cost reductions through anomaly detection (Sarker, 2021). Integration with IoT and big data frameworks enabled real-time monitoring at scale.
Retail and E-Commerce deployed recommendation systems using collaborative filtering and clustering to personalize consumer experiences, with real-world deployments reporting 14–20% efficiency gains in supply chain management. Natural language processing enhanced sentiment analysis and customer feedback mining.
Agriculture benefited from precision farming applications using ML on IoT and satellite data for yield prediction, disease detection, and soil management. Random forests and artificial neural networks achieved measurable RMSE reductions in crop forecasting, supporting sustainable practices aligned with Agriculture 5.0 frameworks.
Education applied DM and ML to learning analytics, student performance prediction, dropout detection, and personalized learning pathways. Generative AI tools appeared in approximately 30% of recent education studies, though practical skill assessment gaps and ethical concerns regarding student surveillance remained underaddressed.
Public Administration and Policy used ML for crime prediction, tax fraud detection, social service targeting, and policy evaluation. While ML enhanced administrative efficiency, studies highlighted persistent mismatches between technical optimization objectives and public value outcomes (Esteva et al., 2017).
Cybersecurity employed anomaly detection via classification and clustering on network traffic data to prevent intrusions and system compromises, with smart city applications extending ML to energy optimization and traffic management.


4. Emerging Trends
Several notable trends emerged across domains. First, deep learning dominance for unstructured data (images, text, sensor streams) increasingly outperformed traditional ML approaches. Second, explainable AI (XAI) became a critical research focus as stakeholders in high-stakes domains demanded interpretable predictions from black-box models (Arrieta et al., 2020). Third, federated learning emerged as a privacy-preserving approach enabling model training across decentralized data sources without sharing raw data — particularly relevant in healthcare (Li et al., 2020). Fourth, generative AI and MLOps frameworks for production deployment and model monitoring gained traction across sectors. Fifth, multimodal data fusion combining text, images, and sensor data increasingly outperformed single-modality approaches.


5. Research Gaps
Despite substantial progress, several gaps persist. First, most studies were conducted in high-resource settings with large, well-labeled datasets, limiting generalizability to low-resource environments. Second, few articles assessed post-implementation outcomes or organizational decision quality, leaving deployment effectiveness largely unevaluated. Third, human-AI interaction — how practitioners interpret, trust, or override model outputs — received limited empirical attention. Fourth, ethical and governance frameworks remained largely conceptual rather than empirically tested, particularly regarding algorithmic bias, data privacy, and accountability in automated decision-making.


6. Proposed hypothesis
Based on the synthesized evidence, the following hypothesis is proposed:
H1: In real-world organizational settings, the integration of explainable AI (XAI) frameworks (e.g., SHAP, LIME) into machine learning predictive models will increase clinician adoption rates by at least 30% compared to equivalent non-interpretable black-box models, as measured by validated trust scales, system usage logs, and decision accuracy metrics in a multi-center randomized controlled trial conducted over 12 months.
This hypothesis is directly falsifiable, addresses the interpretability gap identified across multiple domains, and specifies a concrete research design for empirical validation.


7. Conclusion
This systematic literature review demonstrates that data mining and machine learning have achieved substantial real-world impact across healthcare, finance, manufacturing, retail, agriculture, education, public administration, and cybersecurity. However, critical gaps in generalizability, interpretability, and ethical governance must be addressed to realize their full potential. Future research should prioritize hybrid human-AI decision systems, cross-domain model generalization, longitudinal deployment evaluation, and equitable governance frameworks. By addressing these gaps, the next generation of DM and ML applications can move beyond prediction toward transparent, accountable, and impactful decision-making systems.


References
Alhumaidi, H., et al. (2025). The use of machine learning for analyzing real-world data in clinical decision support. Journal of Medical Systems, 49(1), 1–18.
Arrieta, A. B., et al. (2020). Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI): Concepts, taxonomies, opportunities, and challenges toward responsible AI. Information Fusion, 58, 82–115.
Dal Pozzolo, A., et al. (2015). Adversarial drift detection in the real world. IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems, 26(11), 2872–2885.
Esteva, A., et al. (2017). Dermatologist-level classification of skin cancer with deep neural networks. Nature, 542(7639), 115–118.
Han, J., Kamber, M., & Pei, J. (2012). Data Mining: Concepts and Techniques (3rd ed.). Morgan Kaufmann.
Li, T., et al. (2020). Federated learning: Challenges, methods, and future directions. IEEE Signal Processing Magazine, 37(3), 50–60.
Moher, D., et al. (2009). Preferred reporting items for systematic reviews and meta-analyses (PRISMA). PLoS Medicine, 6(7), e1000097.
Page, M. J., et al. (2021). The PRISMA 2020 statement: An updated guideline for reporting systematic reviews. BMJ, 372, n71.
Rajpurkar, P., et al. (2022). AI in health and medicine. Nature Medicine, 28(1), 31–38.
Ricci, F., Rokach, L., & Shapira, B. (2011). Introduction to Recommender Systems Handbook. Springer.
Sarker, I. H. (2021). Machine learning: Algorithms, real-world applications and research directions. SN Computer Science, 2(3), 160.

title: Assignment 02: Prompt Exercise
text:
Reflection

Each model approaches the systematic review differently
Each model brought a distinct approach to the systematic literature review task. ChatGPT prioritized breadth, covering six domains with a well-developed discussion section connecting findings to broader implications. However, its citation practices were inconsistent — hyperlinked references replaced formal APA citations, and a non-English word appeared in the text, suggesting occasional generation errors. Copilot demonstrated the strongest structural discipline, explicitly referencing PRISMA guidelines from Moher et al. (2009) and maintaining the most formally organized review format. Its weakness was a narrower domain scope and limited empirical specificity. Grok 3 stood out for its empirical precision — it reported specific performance metrics (AUROCs, MSE reductions, efficiency gains) and covered eight domains including agriculture and cybersecurity, which the other models overlooked. Its PRISMA 2020 reference was the most current. However, its reference list was the weakest, with most citations described as “available upon request.”


Prompt refinements yielded the best results for each model
For ChatGPT, requiring APA-formatted, verifiable citations and specifying a publication date range (2018–2024) substantially improved academic rigor and eliminated the citation inconsistencies. For Copilot, mandating coverage of at least six domains including an emerging area, along with requiring specific performance metrics, resolved its tendency toward structural precision at the expense of empirical depth. For Grok 3, the most effective refinement was requiring a minimum of ten verifiable APA references — this addressed its most significant weakness while preserving its strengths in empirical detail and innovative trend coverage.


Learn about leveraging AI for structured academic reviews
This exercise demonstrated that no single AI model is uniformly superior across all dimensions of a systematic literature review. Each model exhibited a distinct profile of strengths and weaknesses that could be strategically leveraged through targeted prompt engineering. The most important insight was that prompt specificity directly determines output quality — vague prompts produce generic responses, while detailed, criteria-driven prompts yield academically rigorous content. The cross-model synthesis produced a final review that was demonstrably stronger than any individual model output, combining Copilot’s structural rigor, ChatGPT’s discussion depth, and Grok 3’s empirical precision. This points to a promising workflow for AI-assisted academic research: deploy multiple models in parallel, evaluate outputs critically against explicit criteria, and synthesize deliberately. Future use of AI for literature reviews should also prioritize citation verification as a non-negotiable quality check, regardless of which model is used.

section: US Department of Defense Reports on China (1999–2025)
text:
US Department of Defense Reports on China (1999–2025)

Load and build corpus from PDFs


Code
# Set path to USDOD folder
dod_path <- "USDOD/USDOD/"

# List all PDF files
pdf_files <- list.files(dod_path, pattern = "\\.pdf$|\\.PDF$",
                        full.names = TRUE, ignore.case = TRUE)

# Extract year from filenames
years <- str_extract(basename(pdf_files), "^[0-9]{4}")

# Remove files where year could not be extracted
valid <- !is.na(years)
pdf_files <- pdf_files[valid]
years <- years[valid]

# Read PDF text
dod_texts <- map_chr(pdf_files, function(f) {
  tryCatch(
    paste(pdf_text(f), collapse = " "),
    error = function(e) ""
  )
})

# Build corpus
corp_dod <- corpus(dod_texts,
                   docnames = paste0("DOD_", years),
                   docvars = data.frame(year = as.integer(years)))

summary(corp_dod)

title: Assignment 04: Text Analytics using quanteda
text:
Wordfish
Wordfish is an unsupervised text scaling model used to estimate the ideological or positional placement of documents along a single latent dimension — without requiring any reference texts or prior labels. It was developed by Slapin and Proksch (2008) and is widely used in political science to scale party manifestos, legislative speeches, and policy documents.
The model assumes that word counts follow a Poisson distribution and estimates:

θ (theta): The document position on the latent dimension (e.g., hawkish vs. dovish, early vs. late)
β (beta): The word-level discrimination parameter — how strongly each word is associated with one end of the dimension
α (alpha): Document fixed effects (document length)
ψ (psi): Word fixed effects (overall word frequency)

In the context of DOD reports, Wordfish can reveal how the rhetorical framing of China’s military threat has shifted across administrations and years.

Run Wordfish on DOD reports


Code
# Trim low-frequency terms for stability
dfm_dod_trim <- dfm_trim(dfm_dod, min_termfreq = 5, min_docfreq = 2)

# Run Wordfish
set.seed(6323)
wf_model <- textmodel_wordfish(dfm_dod_trim)
summary(wf_model)

title: Assignment 04: Text Analytics using quanteda
section: Comparing positions- Wordfish and Scaling Methods
text:
Comparing positions- Wordfish and Scaling Methods
Position comparison across documents can be achieved using several scaling methods in quanteda:

Method 1: Wordfish (Unsupervised Scaling)
Already run above. Wordfish places each DOD report on a single latent dimension based purely on word usage patterns. Documents at opposite ends of the θ scale represent the most rhetorically distinct reports — useful for tracking how threat framing has evolved without imposing any prior assumptions.


Method 2: Wordscores (Supervised Scaling)
Wordscores requires reference texts with known positions. For DOD reports, we can assign reference scores to the earliest (1999) and most recent (2025) reports as anchors, then estimate positions of all other reports.


Code
# Assign reference scores: 1999 = -1 (baseline), 2025 = +1 (most recent)
ref_scores <- rep(NA, ndoc(dfm_dod_trim))
ref_scores[docvars(dfm_dod_trim, "year") == min(docvars(dfm_dod_trim, "year"))] <- -1
ref_scores[docvars(dfm_dod_trim, "year") == max(docvars(dfm_dod_trim, "year"))] <- 1

ws_model <- textmodel_wordscores(dfm_dod_trim, y = ref_scores)

# Predict scores for all documents
ws_pred <- predict(ws_model, se.fit = TRUE, newdata = dfm_dod_trim)

ws_positions <- data.frame(
  year = docvars(dfm_dod_trim, "year"),
  score = ws_pred$fit,
  se = ws_pred$se.fit
)

ggplot(ws_positions, aes(x = year, y = score)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "#e74c3c") +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = score - 1.96 * se,
                    ymax = score + 1.96 * se),
                width = 0.5, color = "#e74c3c", alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_line(color = "#e74c3c", alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
  labs(title = "Wordscores Document Positions: DOD China Reports",
       subtitle = "Supervised scaling anchored at 1999 (−1) and 2025 (+1)",
       x = "Year", y = "Estimated Score") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)











Method 3: Comparing Wordfish vs. Wordscores


Code
# Combine both estimates for comparison
comparison_df <- data.frame(
  year = wf_positions$year,
  Wordfish = scale(wf_positions$theta)[, 1],
  Wordscores = scale(ws_positions$score)[, 1]
) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c("Wordfish", "Wordscores"),
               names_to = "method", values_to = "position")

ggplot(comparison_df, aes(x = year, y = position, color = method)) +
  geom_line(size = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Wordfish" = "#2980b9", "Wordscores" = "#e74c3c")) +
  labs(title = "Comparing Wordfish vs. Wordscores Positions",
       subtitle = "DOD China Reports (1999–2025), both scaled to z-scores",
       x = "Year", y = "Scaled Position", color = "Method") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)











Interpretation
The comparison plot reveals how both methods track the rhetorical evolution of DOD reports on China’s military power over time. Reports from the early 2000s tend to cluster toward one end of the positional spectrum, reflecting a relatively measured tone in the post-Cold War period. Reports from the 2010s onward show a consistent shift, reflecting growing concern over China’s military modernization, cyber capabilities, space programs, and assertiveness in the Taiwan Strait. Wordfish captures this shift unsupervisedly through pure word patterns, while Wordscores confirms it through supervised anchoring. The convergence of both methods strengthens confidence in the observed trend.

title: Reflection Class 03: AI, Originality, AGI, and Scientific Research
text: AI complicates traditional ideas of originality by shifting the focus from producing content to curating, interpreting, and refining ideas. While AI can assist with drafting or coding, the originality may lie in how researchers frame questions, evaluate outputs, and make normative judgments. From this perspective, AI does not eliminate originality but changes what counts as original intellectual contribution.

title: Reflection Class 03: AI, Originality, AGI, and Scientific Research
text:
AGI and Scientific Research
Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) refers to systems capable of performing a wide range of cognitive tasks at or beyond human levels. If developed, AGI could dramatically accelerate scientific discovery by automating data analysis and hypothesis generation. However, it also raises concerns about epistemic authority, transparency, and the role of human judgment in research.

title: Reflection 03 (Class 7): Prediction, Explanation, and Causal Reasoning
text:
Prediction asks: What will happen? Explanation asks: Why does it happen?
When explanation matters: Policy design requires understanding mechanisms. If a government wants to reduce public distrust in digital services, knowing distrust correlates with low digital literacy is insufficient — policymakers must know whether digital literacy causes distrust or whether both are driven by age or socioeconomic status.
When prediction suffices: Operational decisions often need only accurate forecasts. A city allocating road maintenance resources needs to know which roads will fail — not why deterioration occurs.
Research example: For e-government adoption and citizen satisfaction, predictive models identify who will use digital services. But designing services that increase trust requires explanatory models identifying which interface features drive satisfaction, controlling for confounders.
