title: Reflection 03 (Class 7): Prediction, Explanation, and Causal Reasoning
section: Causal diagram: Technology adoption and public trust
text:
Causal diagram: Technology adoption and public trust
The diagram below illustrates a relationship central to my research in Technology and Public Management.


Code
library(ggplot2)

nodes <- data.frame(
  x = c(1, 5, 3, 3, 3),
  y = c(3, 3, 5, 1, 3),
  label = c("E-Government\nAdoption", "Public Trust\nin Government",
            "Digital\nLiteracy", "Socioeconomic\nStatus",
            "Perceived\nService Quality"),
  type = c("treatment", "outcome", "confounder", "confounder", "mediator")
)

edges <- data.frame(
  x    = c(1, 1, 3, 3, 3, 3),
  y    = c(3, 3, 5, 5, 1, 1),
  xend = c(3, 5, 1, 5, 1, 5),
  yend = c(3, 3, 3, 3, 3, 3)
)

colors <- c("treatment" = "#2980b9", "outcome" = "#27ae60",
            "confounder" = "#e74c3c", "mediator" = "#16a085")

ggplot() +
  geom_segment(data = edges,
               aes(x = x, y = y, xend = xend, yend = yend),
               arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.3, "cm"), type = "closed"),
               color = "gray50", linewidth = 0.8, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_point(data = nodes,
             aes(x = x, y = y, color = type),
             size = 18, alpha = 0.9) +
  geom_text(data = nodes,
            aes(x = x, y = y, label = label),
            size = 3.5, fontface = "bold", color = "white",
            lineheight = 0.9) +
  scale_color_manual(values = colors,
                     labels = c("Confounder", "Mediator", "Outcome", "Treatment")) +
  labs(title = "Causal Diagram: E-Government Adoption and Public Trust",
       subtitle = "Technology and Public Management Research Area",
       color = "Node Type") +
  theme_void(base_size = 14) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0.5),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5, color = "gray50"),
        legend.position = "bottom")










Key variables

Treatment: E-Government Technology Adoption
Outcome: Public Trust in Government
Mediator: Perceived Service Quality
Confounders: Digital Literacy, Socioeconomic Status



Distinguishing causal claims from predictive associations
A predictive association establishes that adoption and trust co-occur. This could arise from true causation, reverse causation, or confounding. To establish causation:

RCTs: Randomly assign citizens to use or not use e-government services
Instrumental variables: Use exogenous broadband infrastructure variation as an instrument
Difference-in-Differences: Compare trust changes across jurisdictions adopting at different times
SEM: Explicitly model the mediating role of perceived service quality

title: Reflection 05 (Class 9): LLMs - Capabilities, Failures, and the Future of Research
text:
Large language models have demonstrated remarkable capabilities across a wide range of knowledge-intensive tasks. Drawing on the transformer architecture introduced by Vaswani et al. (2017) and the bidirectional pre-training paradigm of BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), modern LLMs have achieved near-human or superhuman performance on several classes of problems:
Text understanding and generation: LLMs excel at summarizing documents, generating coherent long-form text, translating between languages, and reformulating ideas across registers and audiences. For researchers, this translates into powerful tools for literature synthesis, draft generation, and communication.
In-context reasoning: With chain-of-thought prompting, LLMs can solve multi-step reasoning problems, perform mathematical calculations, and work through logical arguments in a stepwise manner, capabilities that were largely absent in earlier language models.
Information retrieval and synthesis: When grounded with retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) - as demonstrated by Lewis et al. (2020) - LLMs can combine parametric knowledge (stored in model weights) with non-parametric knowledge (retrieved from external documents), substantially improving factual accuracy on knowledge-intensive tasks.
Code generation and debugging: LLMs can write, explain, and debug code across programming languages, significantly accelerating computational research workflows, including data cleaning, analysis scripting, and visualization.
Few-shot and zero-shot generalization: Unlike traditional supervised models that require large labeled datasets for each new task, LLMs can perform new tasks from a small number of examples or even a natural language description alone, enabling rapid prototyping of analytical pipelines.

title: Reflection 05 (Class 9): LLMs - Capabilities, Failures, and the Future of Research
section: Generative AI and Public Administration research in the next five years
text:
Generative AI and Public Administration research in the next five years

Discipline: Public Administration and Behavioral Public Administration
Public administration as a field sits at the intersection of political science, organizational theory, psychology, and policy analysis. Its research methods range from quantitative survey analysis and administrative data mining to qualitative case studies and ethnographic fieldwork. Generative AI will reshape this discipline unevenly - transforming some research tasks dramatically while others must remain fundamentally human-driven.



Research tasks that will change most
1. Literature synthesis and gap identification: The volume of published research in public administration has grown beyond what any individual researcher can systematically track. Within five years, AI-assisted literature synthesis tools combining LLMs with RAG over indexed academic databases will become standard practice for scoping reviews, meta-analyses, and research proposal development. What currently takes months of reading and note-taking may be compressed into days of AI-assisted synthesis followed by targeted close reading.
2. Automated coding of qualitative data: A substantial proportion of public administration research relies on qualitative data: open-ended survey responses, interview transcripts, policy documents, and legislative records. LLM-based coding assistance, in which the model proposes thematic codes that human researchers verify and refine, will significantly reduce the labor cost of qualitative analysis while potentially improving inter-rater reliability through standardized prompting.
3. Administrative data analysis and pattern detection: Governments generate enormous volumes of administrative data, procurement records, service delivery logs, compliance filings, and citizen interaction records that are currently underutilized due to analytical capacity constraints. NLP pipelines and LLMs will enable researchers to mine these datasets for patterns in bureaucratic behavior, service delivery equity, and institutional responsiveness that are currently invisible.
4. Citizen sentiment and trust monitoring: LLM-powered sentiment analysis of social media, e-participation platforms, and digital government service reviews will enable near-real-time monitoring of public trust and satisfaction with government institutions, transforming what is currently a slow, expensive survey-based process into a continuous, high-frequency analytical capability.
5. Cross-lingual comparative research: Public administration is inherently comparative, but language barriers limit most researchers to English-language literature and cases. LLMs with strong multilingual capabilities will enable researchers to engage with non-English administrative documents, policy texts, and citizen feedback, substantially broadening the empirical base of comparative public administration.



What should remain human-driven
1. Normative and ethical judgment: Public administration is fundamentally a normative discipline; it is concerned not only with what governments do but with what they should do. Questions about equity, democratic accountability, public value, and the ethics of governance cannot be delegated to AI systems that have no stake in the outcomes and no capacity for genuine moral reasoning. Researchers must retain the interpretive and evaluative authority to make normative claims.
2. Research design and causal inference: Designing valid research specifying causal mechanisms, selecting appropriate identification strategies, and anticipating confounders requires deep contextual knowledge, theoretical grounding, and scientific judgment that LLMs cannot reliably provide. AI can assist with implementing a chosen design, but the design itself must remain human-driven.
3. Fieldwork and contextual knowledge production: Ethnographic research, interviews, and participatory observation produce forms of knowledge, a tacit understanding of organizational culture, trust-based relationships with research participants, and sensitivity to irreducibly human power dynamics. Generative AI cannot conduct fieldwork, build rapport, or navigate the ethical complexities of research relationships.
4. Peer review and quality control: The evaluation of research contributions assessing theoretical originality, methodological rigor, and substantive significance requires expert judgment that cannot be automated without degrading the quality of the scholarly record. As AI-generated text becomes more prevalent, human peer review becomes more rather than less important.
5. Accountability for research claims: Regardless of how AI tools assist in the research process, intellectual and ethical accountability for published claims must remain with human researchers. The growing use of AI in research makes transparent disclosure of AI contributions more important, not less, and the responsibility for errors, omissions, and harms rests with the authors, not the tools they used.



A personal research perspective
For my own research on public trust in government and e-government adoption, generative AI will most substantially change how I collect and analyze citizen feedback data, synthesize interdisciplinary literature, and pre-test survey instruments. However, the core intellectual contributions theorizing the mechanisms through which technology shapes trust, designing causal identification strategies, and making normative arguments about what transparent and accountable digital government should look like must remain mine. AI is a powerful research assistant. It is not, and should not become, a substitute for the researcher.

title: Reflection 07 (Class 11): Knowledge Graphs and Research Domain Mapping
text:
A knowledge graph is a structured representation of knowledge in which entities (nodes) and the relationships between them (edges) are explicitly encoded in a graph format. Each node represents a real-world object, concept, or event a person, organization, policy, institution, or idea and each edge represents a named, typed relationship between two nodes. For example: [Citizen] –trusts–> [Government Institution], or [E-Government Platform] –increases–> [Service Quality].
Knowledge graphs originated in semantic web research and were popularized at scale by Google’s Knowledge Graph (2012), which powers the factual panels in search results. Since then, they have been adopted across biomedical research, financial intelligence, and social science as a way of representing complex, interconnected domain knowledge in a machine-readable and queryable format.

title: Reflection 07 (Class 11): Knowledge Graphs and Research Domain Mapping
section: Knowledge graph for my research domain
text:
Knowledge graph for my research domain

Research Domain: Public Trust, E-Government Adoption, and Behavioral Public Administration
My research examines how citizens’ behavioral responses to digital government technologies shape their trust in public institutions. The following knowledge graph identifies three core entities and three relationships that structure this domain.


Three Entities

Citizen: An individual who interacts with government services, forms attitudes toward public institutions, and makes behavioral decisions about adopting or avoiding digital government platforms
E-Government Platform: A digital interface through which government services are delivered to citizens (e.g., online tax filing, digital ID systems, e-participation portals, social welfare applications)
Government Institution: A public organization responsible for designing, deploying, and administering government services and digital infrastructure (e.g., tax authority, social services agency, municipal government)



Three Relationships

[Citizen] –trusts–> [Government Institution]: Citizens hold varying levels of institutional trust toward specific government organizations, shaped by their prior experiences, values, and perceptions of institutional competence, integrity, and benevolence
[Citizen] –adopts–> [E-Government Platform]: Citizens make behavioral adoption decisions about digital government services, influenced by perceived usefulness, ease of use, digital literacy, and trust in the deploying institution
[E-Government Platform] –deployed_by–> [Government Institution]: Digital platforms are designed, deployed, and maintained by specific government institutions, whose reputation, communication style, and service quality shape citizen perceptions of those platforms



Knowledge graph visualization


Code
library(ggplot2)

# Nodes
nodes <- data.frame(
  x = c(1, 5, 9),
  y = c(3, 6, 3),
  label = c("Citizen", "E-Government\nPlatform", "Government\nInstitution"),
  type = c("citizen", "platform", "institution")
)

# Edges with labels
edges <- data.frame(
  x    = c(1,   1,   5),
  y    = c(3,   3,   6),
  xend = c(5,   9,   9),
  yend = c(6,   3,   3),
  label = c("adopts", "trusts", "deployed_by")
)

# Midpoints for edge labels
edges$mx <- (edges$x + edges$xend) / 2
edges$my <- (edges$y + edges$yend) / 2

colors <- c("citizen" = "#2980b9",
            "platform" = "#27ae60",
            "institution" = "#e74c3c")

ggplot() +
  geom_segment(data = edges,
               aes(x = x, y = y, xend = xend, yend = yend),
               arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.35, "cm"), type = "closed"),
               color = "gray50", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_label(data = edges,
             aes(x = mx, y = my, label = label),
             size = 4, fontface = "italic",
             fill = "white", color = "gray30",
             label.size = 0.3) +
  geom_point(data = nodes,
             aes(x = x, y = y, color = type),
             size = 22, alpha = 0.92) +
  geom_text(data = nodes,
            aes(x = x, y = y, label = label),
            size = 4, fontface = "bold", color = "white",
            lineheight = 0.9) +
  scale_color_manual(values = colors) +
  labs(title = "Knowledge Graph: Public Trust and E-Government Adoption",
       subtitle = "Technology and Public Management — Behavioral Public Administration") +
  theme_void(base_size = 13) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0.5, size = 14),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5, color = "gray50", size = 11),
        legend.position = "none")












How this structure helps answer questions, a flat dataset cannot
A flat dataset in this research domain would typically contain rows representing individual citizens, with columns for demographic variables, adoption behavior, and trust scores. While this supports regression analysis and descriptive statistics, it cannot answer several classes of questions that the knowledge graph structure enables:
1. Path-dependent questions: “Does the pathway from citizen adoption to institutional trust differ depending on which specific e-government platform was used and which institution deployed it?”
A flat dataset with aggregate adoption and trust scores cannot distinguish between pathways it can only test whether adoption and trust are correlated. The knowledge graph encodes the specific platform-institution deployment relationships, enabling analysis of whether trust effects are platform-specific or institution-specific.
2. Indirect relationship questions: “Are citizens who trust high-performing institutions more likely to adopt platforms deployed by those institutions, and does that adoption in turn reinforce trust?”
This question involves a feedback loop, trust influences adoption, which influences trust that a static flat dataset cannot represent. The knowledge graph can encode longitudinal or dynamic relationship updates that model reciprocal causation.
3. Missing relationship detection: “Which government institutions have deployed e-government platforms but have no citizen adoption records in high-trust segments?”
This is a graph query about the absence of expected relationships, a class of questions that is straightforward in graph traversal but extremely difficult in tabular SQL queries.
4. Network centrality and influence: “Which e-government platforms are most central to the trust network, meaning their adoption or failure has the greatest downstream effect on institutional trust across the broadest range of citizens?”
Network centrality measures (degree, betweenness, PageRank) applied to the knowledge graph can identify structurally influential entities that would be invisible in a flat dataset focused on individual-level correlations.
5. Cross-domain integration: The knowledge graph can be extended to integrate entities from other data sources policy documents, administrative performance records, and social media sentiment linking them to the same citizen, platform, and institution nodes. A flat dataset would require complex merging operations that often lose relational context; the knowledge graph preserves it by design.

title: Reflection (Class 5): Knowledge, Information, and AI Failures
text:
Academic reading in a PhD program is not passive consumption; it is an active, structured process of building knowledge that accumulates across courses, papers, and research projects. I have developed the following reading routine to maximize retention and analytical utility:
Before reading: - Skim the abstract, introduction, and conclusion first to establish the paper’s central argument and contribution before engaging with the details - Write down one question I want the paper to answer. This focuses attention and prevents passive reading - Check the citation count and venue to calibrate how much weight to give the paper in the broader literature
During reading: - Annotate directly in the PDF using three categories: key claims (highlighted), methodological notes (underlined), and questions/critiques (margin notes) - For quantitative papers, pay special attention to identification strategies, sample characteristics, and robustness checks for theoretical papers, map the logical structure of what assumptions lead to what conclusions
After reading: - Write a 3-sentence summary: (1) what the paper argues, (2) what evidence it provides, and (3) what gap or question it leaves open - Add the paper to my Zotero library with tags for research area, method, and theoretical contribution - Note connections to other papers I have read. Knowledge accumulates through relationships between ideas, not isolated facts



For text analysis, I am interested in analyzing government AI use case inventories, specifically the OMB Consolidated AI Use Case Inventories from 2022 to 2025. These documents contain structured and unstructured descriptions of how federal agencies are deploying AI, making them ideal for text mining to track how governance language, risk framing, and transparency disclosure have evolved.
Data source: OMB AI Use Case Inventories (publicly available at ai.gov and data.gov)
How to get the data: The inventories are available as downloadable CSV and Excel files. Text fields describing AI use cases can be extracted and analyzed using tidytext in R for topic modeling, keyword frequency analysis, and sentiment analysis of risk and accountability language.

title: Reflection (Class 5): Knowledge, Information, and AI Failures
section: How Do Humans Find Knowledge? How Is Knowledge Different from Information?
text:
How Do Humans Find Knowledge? How Is Knowledge Different from Information?

The Distinction Between Information and Knowledge
The distinction between information and knowledge is foundational to knowledge mining as a field and to understanding both the power and the limitations of AI systems that process information at scale.
Information is structured, transmissible data that reduces uncertainty about a state of the world. A weather report that says “temperature: 72°F” is information. A database record showing that a citizen filed a tax return online is information. Information can be stored, transmitted, copied, and processed without being understood. It is syntactic; it has form and structure, but it is not inherently meaningful.
Knowledge is information that has been interpreted, contextualized, and integrated into a framework of understanding that enables reasoning, judgment, and action. Knowing that it is 72°F is useful, but knowing that 72°F in Dallas in January is unusually warm and understanding what that implies for seasonal patterns, agricultural planning, or energy consumption is knowledge. Knowledge is semantic; it carries meaning, and it is relational: it exists in connection with other knowledge, not in isolation.
The philosopher Edmund Gettier famously challenged the classical definition of knowledge as “justified true belief,” demonstrating that the relationship between evidence and knowledge is more complex than simple justification. For our purposes, what matters is that knowledge involves not just having information but understanding its significance, its relationships, and its implications for action.


How Humans Find Knowledge
Humans acquire knowledge through several interconnected processes that are qualitatively different from information retrieval:
1. Pattern recognition across experience. Humans identify patterns by accumulating experience over time and recognizing regularities that recur across different contexts. A public administrator who has observed many bureaucratic reform initiatives develops a pattern recognition capacity, knowledge about which reforms tend to succeed, which tend to fail, and why that cannot be fully articulated as explicit rules but informs judgment reliably.
2. Abductive reasoning: Humans generate knowledge through abductive reasoning from incomplete evidence to the most plausible explanation. When a researcher observes that citizens who use e-government services report higher institutional trust, they do not simply record this correlation as information; they reason about the most plausible causal mechanism and generate a hypothesis that can be tested. This inferential leap from data to explanation is a distinctly human form of knowledge generation.
3. Social and collaborative construction Much human knowledge is socially constructed through dialogue, debate, peer review, and collective sense-making. Scientific knowledge in particular is produced through communities of practice that evaluate evidence, challenge claims, and reach provisional consensus. This social process filters out errors and biases that individual reasoners might miss.
4. Embodied and tacit knowledge. Following Michael Polanyi’s concept of tacit knowledge, “we know more than we can tell,” much human knowledge is embodied in practice, skill, and intuition rather than explicit propositions. An experienced researcher’s sense of when a finding “doesn’t feel right,” or a clinician’s intuition about a patient’s condition, represents knowledge that cannot be fully reduced to information.
5. Integration and synthesis Humans find knowledge by connecting disparate pieces of information into coherent frameworks, theories, models, and narratives that make sense of complex phenomena. This synthetic capacity is what distinguishes knowledge from mere information accumulation.


Implications for AI and Knowledge Mining
AI systems, including large language models, are extraordinarily powerful information processors. They can retrieve, summarize, classify, and generate text at scales and speeds that far exceed human capacity. But they do not, in the full sense described above, know things. They process statistical patterns in training data without the embodied experience, abductive reasoning, social accountability, or genuine understanding that characterize human knowledge.
This is not merely a philosophical point it has practical implications for how AI systems should be used in research. AI is best understood as a powerful tool for information processing that can assist human knowledge construction, not a replacement for the reasoning, judgment, and interpretive capacity that knowledge requires.

title: Reflection (Class 5): Knowledge, Information, and AI Failures
section: AI Discrepancies and Failures: A Catalog and Pattern Analysis
text:
AI Discrepancies and Failures: A Catalog and Pattern Analysis
The following list documents specific AI failures and discrepancies I have encountered or identified through research, organized by failure type.

Hallucination (Factual Fabrication)

Citation fabrication: Multiple LLMs have generated fully formatted academic citation author names, journal titles, volume numbers, and page ranges that correspond to no real publication. The citations appear syntactically correct and topically plausible, making them dangerous for researchers who do not verify independently.
Biographical errors: LLMs have generated confident but incorrect biographical details about real researchers, wrong institutional affiliations, incorrect publication dates, and fabricated awards.
Statistical confabulation: When asked about specific statistics (e.g., “what percentage of federal agencies use AI for benefits determination?”), LLMs sometimes generate precise-sounding numbers that are not traceable to any real source.



Reasoning Errors

Conjunction fallacy: LLMs sometimes assign higher probability to specific conjunctions than to their components, a well-known human reasoning bias that appears to be replicated in model outputs.
Causal confusion: Models frequently conflate correlation with causation, presenting observational associations as causal claims without acknowledging the identification problem.
Inconsistency across prompts: The same factual question posed with slightly different wording can produce contradictory answers from the same model in the same session.



Bias and Representation Failures

Western-centric defaults: When asked about government institutions, public administration practices, or cultural norms without specifying a context, models default to Western (primarily American or European) frameworks, producing systematically biased outputs for non-Western research contexts.
Recency bias: Models trained on large web corpora overrepresent recent, English-language, high-visibility sources, systematically underrepresenting older scholarship, non-English literature, and work published in lower-prestige venues.
Gender and racial bias: Text generation tasks involving professional roles show that systematic bias models more frequently associate certain professional roles with specific demographic groups in ways that reflect historical biases in training data.



Temporal and Knowledge Cutoff Failures

Outdated information presented as current: Models present information from their training cutoff as current without flagging that developments may have occurred since training, particularly problematic for rapidly evolving fields like AI governance and public sector technology policy.
Inability to acknowledge uncertainty about recency: When asked about recent events, models sometimes confabulate plausible-sounding recent developments rather than acknowledging that they may not have reliable information.



Pattern Analysis
Several patterns emerge from this catalog:
Confidence without calibration is the most pervasive failure pattern. LLMs generate all outputs with a similar apparent confidence level, regardless of whether they are recalling well-established facts or confabulating uncertain details. This miscalibration is the root cause of many downstream failures. Hallucinated citations look identical to real ones because the model presents both with equal certainty.
Statistical pattern matching without semantic understanding underlies most reasoning errors. Models generate outputs that are statistically consistent with patterns in training data but do not reflect a genuine understanding of the logical relationships between concepts. The model produces text that looks like correct reasoning without the underlying inferential process that would make it reliable.
Training data reflects human biases, including geographic, linguistic, demographic, and temporal biases, that are replicated and sometimes amplified in model outputs. AI systems do not eliminate human bias; they encode and scale it.
The practical implication for researchers is consistent: treat AI outputs as a starting point that requires critical evaluation, independent verification, and contextual judgment, not as authoritative information that can be trusted without scrutiny.
